question: What is inheritance in Java? Explain with a simple example.
mainConcepts: Inheritance is an OOP mechanism where a new class acquires properties and behaviors of an existing class
The keyword extends is used to inherit from a parent class
The parent/superclass is the class being inherited from
The child/subclass is the class that inherits
Inheritance promotes code reuse and establishes an IS-A relationship
A subclass can override parent methods to provide specific behavior

question: In OOP, how does Java achieve Encapsulation?
mainConcepts: Encapsulation is the bundling of data (fields) and methods that operate on that data into a single class
Data hiding is achieved by making fields private
Public getter and setter methods provide controlled access to private fields
Encapsulation protects internal state from unauthorized access and modification
Access modifiers (private, protected, public, default) control visibility
It promotes modularity and makes code easier to maintain

question: What is the difference between abstract class and interface in Java?
mainConcepts: An abstract class can have both abstract (unimplemented) and concrete (implemented) methods
An interface can only have abstract methods (prior to Java 8) and constants
A class can extend only one abstract class but can implement multiple interfaces
Abstract classes use the extends keyword; interfaces use the implements keyword
Interfaces provide full abstraction; abstract classes provide partial abstraction
Interfaces define a contract; abstract classes provide a base implementation

question: What are the four main pillars of Object-Oriented Programming?
mainConcepts: Encapsulation bundles data and methods, hiding internal details using access modifiers
Inheritance allows a class to acquire properties of another class, promoting code reuse
Polymorphism allows one interface to have multiple forms — methods behave differently based on the object
Abstraction hides complex implementation details and shows only essential features
Together these four pillars form the foundation of OOP design
referenceAnswer:
The four main pillars of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) are:

1. ENCAPSULATION: The bundling of data (fields) and methods that operate on that data into a single class, while restricting direct access to the fields. Data is hidden behind public methods (getters/setters). Example: Making fields private and providing public methods to access them.

2. INHERITANCE: A mechanism where a new class (subclass) acquires the properties and behaviors of an existing class (superclass). It promotes code reuse and establishes an IS-A relationship. Example: Dog extends Animal.

3. POLYMORPHISM: The ability of an object to take many forms. One interface can be used for different types of objects, and the same method call can produce different behaviors. Two types:
   - Compile-time (method overloading): Same method name, different parameters
   - Runtime (method overriding): Subclass redefines a parent method

4. ABSTRACTION: Hiding complex implementation details and showing only the essential features of an object. Achieved through abstract classes and interfaces. Example: A car driver uses the steering wheel without knowing the internal mechanics.

These four pillars work together to create modular, maintainable, and reusable code. Encapsulation protects data, inheritance promotes reuse, polymorphism provides flexibility, and abstraction simplifies complexity.

question: Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding in Java.
mainConcepts: Method overloading: same class, same method name, different parameter list (compile-time polymorphism)
Method overriding: subclass redefines a parent class method with the same signature (runtime polymorphism)
Overloading does not require inheritance; overriding requires an IS-A relationship
Overloading is resolved at compile time; overriding is resolved at runtime
Overriding uses the @Override annotation for safety
Return types can differ in overloading but must be same or covariant in overriding

question: Explain polymorphism in Java.
mainConcepts: Polymorphism means "many forms" — the same interface can be used for different underlying data types
Compile-time polymorphism is achieved through method overloading
Runtime polymorphism is achieved through method overriding and dynamic method dispatch
A parent class reference can hold a child class object (upcasting)
The JVM determines at runtime which overridden method to call based on the actual object type
Polymorphism enables writing flexible, extensible, and maintainable code

question: Explain the use of the super keyword in Java.
mainConcepts: super refers to the immediate parent class object
super() calls the parent class constructor — must be the first statement
super.method() calls the parent version of an overridden method
super.field accesses a parent class field hidden by a child class field
Constructor chaining ensures parent class initialization happens before child
If no super() is called, Java automatically inserts super() (no-arg)

question: What is abstraction in Java? How is it implemented?
mainConcepts: Abstraction hides complex implementation details and shows only essential features to the user
It is implemented through abstract classes and interfaces
Abstract classes provide partial abstraction — they can have both abstract and concrete methods
Interfaces provide full abstraction — they define a contract with no implementation (pre-Java 8)
An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly
Abstract methods have no body and must be implemented by subclasses

question: Why is encapsulation considered important in OOP design?
mainConcepts: Encapsulation protects internal object state from unauthorized external access
It allows internal implementation to change without affecting external code
Validation logic in setters prevents invalid data from corrupting object state
It makes debugging easier because all access goes through controlled methods
It promotes loose coupling between components
It supports modularity — each class is a self-contained unit

question: What is the role of the final keyword in Java for classes and methods?
mainConcepts: final class: cannot be extended/inherited (e.g., String class is final)
final method: cannot be overridden by subclasses
final variable: becomes a constant — its value cannot be changed after assignment
final prevents breaking of immutability contracts
final classes ensure security and prevent subclass tampering
final methods preserve the original behavior in inheritance hierarchies

question: Explain with examples how Java supports multiple inheritance using interfaces.
mainConcepts: Java does not support multiple inheritance with classes to avoid the diamond problem
A class can implement multiple interfaces, achieving multiple inheritance of type
Each interface defines a contract that the implementing class must fulfill
From Java 8, interfaces can have default methods, adding complexity
If two interfaces have the same default method, the class must override it to resolve ambiguity
This approach gives the benefits of multiple inheritance without the problems

question: State the difference between a class and an object in Java.
mainConcepts: A class is a blueprint or template; an object is an instance of a class
A class defines properties and behaviors; an object holds actual values
No memory is allocated for fields when a class is declared; memory is allocated when an object is created
Multiple objects can be created from one class, each with its own state
A class is a logical entity; an object is a physical entity in memory
The new keyword creates an object (instantiation)

question: What are getter and setter methods?
mainConcepts: Getter methods (accessors) return the value of a private field
Setter methods (mutators) assign a value to a private field
They are the primary mechanism for implementing encapsulation
Setters can include validation logic before updating the field
Naming convention: getFieldName() and setFieldName()
They provide controlled access to private data

question: How are constructors different from methods in Java?
mainConcepts: A constructor is called when an object is created using the new keyword; methods are called after creation
A constructor has the same name as the class; methods can have any name
Constructors have no return type (not even void); methods must have a return type
A constructor initializes a new object; methods perform operations on existing objects
Java provides a default no-arg constructor if none is defined
Constructors can be overloaded but not overridden

question: What is the purpose of the this keyword in Java? Give an example.
mainConcepts: this is a reference to the current object — the object on which a method or constructor was invoked
this.field distinguishes instance variables from local variables with the same name
this() calls another constructor in the same class (constructor chaining)
this can be passed as an argument to other methods
this resolves variable shadowing when parameter names match field names
this cannot be used in static contexts

question: What are the different types of inheritance supported in Java? Explain with examples.
mainConcepts: Java supports single inheritance (one class extends one parent class)
Java supports multilevel inheritance (grandparent -> parent -> child)
Java supports hierarchical inheritance (one parent, multiple children)
Java does NOT support multiple inheritance with classes (diamond problem)
Multiple inheritance is achieved through interfaces
Java also supports hybrid inheritance through interfaces

question: Explain the four access modifiers in Java with their visibility scope.
mainConcepts: public: accessible from any class in any package
private: accessible only within the same class
protected: accessible within the same package and by subclasses in other packages
default (no modifier): accessible only within the same package
Access modifiers are used to implement encapsulation
A class can only be public or default (not private or protected for top-level classes)
referenceAnswer:
Java has four access modifiers that control the visibility of classes, methods, and fields:

1. PUBLIC:
- Keyword: public
- Visibility: Accessible from ANY class in ANY package
- Widest access level — no restrictions
public class MyClass {
    public int value;  // accessible everywhere
}

2. PRIVATE:
- Keyword: private
- Visibility: Accessible ONLY within the SAME class
- Not visible to subclasses or other classes in the same package
- Used for encapsulation and data hiding
public class MyClass {
    private int secret;  // only MyClass can access it
}

3. PROTECTED:
- Keyword: protected
- Visibility: Accessible within the SAME PACKAGE + by SUBCLASSES in other packages
- More restrictive than public, more permissive than default
- Useful in inheritance hierarchies
package p1;
public class Parent {
    protected int value;  // accessible by subclasses (even in other packages)
}

4. DEFAULT (Package-Private):
- No keyword (no modifier)
- Visibility: Accessible ONLY within the SAME PACKAGE
- Cannot be accessed from other packages, even by subclasses
package p1;
class MyClass {  // default class
    int value;  // default field — same package only
}

SUMMARY TABLE (from most to least restrictive):
Modifier    | Class | Package | Subclass | World
private     |  Yes  |   No    |    No    |  No
default     |  Yes  |   Yes   |    No    |  No
protected   |  Yes  |   Yes   |   Yes    |  No
public      |  Yes  |   Yes   |   Yes    |  Yes

Class-level rules: Top-level classes can only be public or default (package-private). Inner classes can use any modifier.

question: What is exception handling in Java? Explain the try-catch-finally mechanism.
mainConcepts: Exception handling is a mechanism to handle runtime errors gracefully without crashing the program
try block contains code that might throw an exception
catch block handles the exception if one occurs
finally block always executes regardless of whether an exception occurred
A try block can have multiple catch blocks for different exception types
Exceptions are objects that extend the Throwable class

question: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java?
mainConcepts: Checked exceptions are checked at compile time; the compiler forces handling via try-catch or throws
Unchecked exceptions occur at runtime and are not checked by the compiler
Checked exceptions extend Exception (but not RuntimeException)
Unchecked exceptions extend RuntimeException
Examples of checked: IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException
Examples of unchecked: NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, ArithmeticException

question: What is the difference between the throw and throws keywords in Java?
mainConcepts: throw is used to explicitly throw an exception object from code
throws is used in the method signature to declare that the method may throw certain exceptions
throw is followed by an exception object; throws is followed by exception class names
throw appears inside method body; throws appears in the method declaration
A method can throw only one exception at a time but can declare multiple with throws
throws delegates exception handling to the caller

question: How do you create and use a custom exception class in Java?
mainConcepts: A custom exception is created by extending Exception (checked) or RuntimeException (unchecked)
A constructor is defined that calls super() with the error message
Custom exceptions should have meaningful names ending with 'Exception'
They are thrown using the throw keyword
Custom exceptions allow specific, domain-specific error handling
They can include additional fields for more error context

question: What is the try-with-resources statement in Java? Explain with an example.
mainConcepts: try-with-resources automatically closes resources that implement AutoCloseable
Resources are declared in the try statement with parentheses
Introduced in Java 7 to simplify resource management
Resources are closed automatically in reverse order of declaration
No need for explicit finally block to close resources
Multiple resources can be declared, separated by semicolons

question: Explain the concept of static members in Java with examples.
mainConcepts: Static members belong to the class itself, not individual objects
Static variables are shared across all instances of a class
Static methods can be called without creating an object (e.g., Math.sqrt())
Static methods cannot access instance variables directly
Static blocks are used for static initialization
Static members are accessed using the class name (e.g., ClassName.staticMember)

question: What is the String class in Java? Explain key methods and immutability.
mainConcepts: String objects are immutable — once created, their value cannot be changed
String literals are stored in the String pool for memory efficiency
String methods include length(), charAt(), substring(), equals(), toLowerCase(), toUpperCase()
String concatenation with + creates a new String object
StringBuilder/StringBuffer are the mutable alternatives for frequent modifications
The String class is final — cannot be subclassed
referenceAnswer:
The String class in Java is one of the most commonly used classes. It represents a sequence of characters and is part of the java.lang package (automatically imported).

KEY CHARACTERISTICS:

1. IMMUTABILITY:
String objects are immutable — once created, their value cannot be changed. Any operation that seems to modify a String actually creates a new String object.

String s = "Hello";
s = s + " World";  // Does NOT modify "Hello" — creates a new String "Hello World"
// The original "Hello" remains unchanged in memory

2. STRING POOL:
String literals are stored in a special area of heap memory called the String constant pool. If the same string literal appears again, Java reuses the pooled reference.

String s1 = "Java";
String s2 = "Java";     // s2 points to the same object as s1
String s3 = new String("Java");  // s3 creates a NEW object in heap (not pooled)
System.out.println(s1 == s2);     // true (same reference)
System.out.println(s1 == s3);     // false (different references)
System.out.println(s1.equals(s3)); // true (same value)

3. COMMON METHODS:
- length(): returns the number of characters
- charAt(int index): returns the character at the given index
- substring(int begin, int end): returns a substring
- equals(String other): compares values (use this, NOT ==)
- equalsIgnoreCase(String other): case-insensitive comparison
- toLowerCase() / toUpperCase(): case conversion
- trim(): removes leading/trailing whitespace
- replace(char old, char new): replaces occurrences
- split(String regex): splits into String array
- indexOf(String s): returns the first index of substring
- contains(CharSequence s): checks if substring exists

Example:
String str = "  Hello, Java!  ";
System.out.println(str.length());              // 15
System.out.println(str.trim());                // "Hello, Java!"
System.out.println(str.charAt(7));             // 'J'
System.out.println(str.substring(2, 7));       // "Hello"
System.out.println(str.toLowerCase());         // "  hello, java!  "
System.out.println(str.contains("Java"));     // true

4. MUTABLE ALTERNATIVES:
- StringBuilder (not thread-safe, faster)
- StringBuffer (thread-safe, synchronized)

Used when many string modifications are needed:
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
sb.append("Hello");
sb.append(" ");
sb.append("World");
String result = sb.toString();  // "Hello World"

question: What is composition in Java? How does it differ from inheritance?
mainConcepts: Composition represents a HAS-A relationship where one object contains another object
In composition, the contained object's lifecycle depends on the container (strong association)
Inheritance represents an IS-A relationship using the extends keyword
Composition provides tighter encapsulation and looser coupling than inheritance
Composition is more flexible at runtime — behaviors can be swapped dynamically
Favor composition over inheritance is a well-known OOP design principle

question: Explain the Student Directory case study design — what classes are involved and their responsibilities?
mainConcepts: StudentDirectory is the main controller class that manages the application flow
Student is the model class containing student data (name, ID, course, etc.)
StudentDatabase handles all data storage and retrieval operations
Output handles all display/output to the user
Keyboard handles all user input from the console
The design separates data, logic, input, and output into distinct classes

question: What is Swing in Java? Explain JFrame and JPanel with their roles.
mainConcepts: Swing is Java's GUI framework for building desktop applications
JFrame represents the main window with title bar, close, and resize controls
JPanel is a lightweight container used to group and organize components
JPanel is added to JFrame's content pane
Swing components are lightweight (pure Java) compared to AWT (native peers)
JFrame.setVisible(true) makes the window appear on screen

question: How is event handling implemented in Java Swing? Explain the ActionListener interface.
mainConcepts: Java uses the delegation event model: event sources fire events, event listeners handle them
ActionListener is an interface with one method: actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)
Components (JButton, JTextField) are event sources
Listener objects are registered with the source using addActionListener()
When the event occurs (e.g., button click), the listener's actionPerformed() is called
The ActionEvent object provides event details (source, command, modifiers)

question: What are common Swing components and how do you use JTextField, JLabel, and JButton together?
mainConcepts: JLabel displays read-only text or an image
JTextField allows user text input (single line)
JButton triggers an action when clicked
Components are added to a container (JPanel/JFrame)
JTextField.getText() retrieves user input
JLabel.setText() updates the displayed text

question: Explain layout managers in Swing. What is the difference between FlowLayout, BorderLayout, and GridLayout?
mainConcepts: Layout managers automatically arrange components within a container
FlowLayout places components left-to-right in rows (default for JPanel)
BorderLayout divides the container into 5 regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER (default for JFrame)
GridLayout divides the container into equal-sized cells in a grid
Layout managers handle component positioning and resizing automatically
setLayout() is used to change a container's layout manager

question: What is the difference between FileReader/FileWriter and FileInputStream/FileOutputStream in Java?
mainConcepts: FileReader and FileWriter are character streams — used for text files (reading/writing characters)
FileInputStream and FileOutputStream are byte streams — used for binary files (reading/writing bytes)
Character streams handle character encoding and decoding automatically
Byte streams read/write raw bytes without encoding considerations
Character streams extend Reader/Writer; byte streams extend InputStream/OutputStream
Use character streams for .txt, .csv, .html; use byte streams for images, audio, video

question: How do you read from and write to a text file using Scanner and PrintWriter in Java?
mainConcepts: Scanner reads data from a file using hasNextLine() and nextLine()
PrintWriter writes formatted text to a file using println() and printf()
Both must handle FileNotFoundException (checked exception)
Scanner can parse primitive types (nextInt(), nextDouble()) from files
PrintWriter overwrites the file by default; use FileWriter with append mode for appending
Always close both streams after use

question: What is serialization in Java? How do you serialize and deserialize objects?
mainConcepts: Serialization converts an object into a byte stream for storage or network transmission
Deserialization reconstructs the object from the byte stream
A class must implement the Serializable interface (marker interface) to be serializable
ObjectOutputStream.writeObject() serializes an object
ObjectInputStream.readObject() deserializes an object
The transient keyword marks fields that should not be serialized

question: Explain the difference between byte streams and character streams in Java I/O.
mainConcepts: Byte streams (InputStream/OutputStream) handle binary data — raw bytes
Character streams (Reader/Writer) handle text data — characters with encoding
Byte streams are base classes for all I/O in Java (InputStream, OutputStream)
Character streams wrap byte streams and add encoding conversion
Use byte streams for images, audio, video, zip files; use character streams for text files
Both use the decorator pattern — add functionality by wrapping streams
referenceAnswer:
Java I/O has two fundamental types of streams: byte streams and character streams.

BYTE STREAMS:
- Work with raw binary data (bytes)
- Based on abstract classes InputStream and OutputStream
- Read/write in units of 8-bit bytes
- No encoding or character conversion
- Suitable for ALL file types, but especially binary files
- Examples: FileInputStream, FileOutputStream, BufferedInputStream, DataInputStream

FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream("image.jpg");
int b;
while ((b = fis.read()) != -1) {
    // b is an int (0-255) representing a byte
}
fis.close();

CHARACTER STREAMS:
- Work with text data (characters)
- Based on abstract classes Reader and Writer
- Read/write in units of 16-bit Unicode characters
- Handle character encoding conversion automatically
- Only suitable for text files (where character encoding matters)
- Examples: FileReader, FileWriter, BufferedReader, PrintWriter

BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("text.txt"));
String line;
while ((line = br.readLine()) != null) {
    // line is a String (character-based)
}
br.close();

CHARACTER STREAMS WRAP BYTE STREAMS:
Character streams are built on top of byte streams. InputStreamReader/OutputStreamWriter bridge the gap:

FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream("data.txt");       // byte stream
InputStreamReader isr = new InputStreamReader(fis, "UTF-8"); // bridge
BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(isr);                  // add buffering

// Shorthand:
BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(
    new InputStreamReader(
        new FileInputStream("data.txt"), "UTF-8"));

HIERARCHY:
Byte Streams:                              Character Streams:
InputStream (abstract)                     Reader (abstract)
  +-- FileInputStream                         +-- FileReader
  +-- BufferedInputStream                     +-- BufferedReader
  +-- DataInputStream                         +-- InputStreamReader
  +-- ObjectInputStream                          +-- FileReader(internal)

OutputStream (abstract)                     Writer (abstract)
  +-- FileOutputStream                        +-- FileWriter
  +-- BufferedOutputStream                    +-- BufferedWriter
  +-- DataOutputStream                        +-- PrintWriter
  +-- ObjectOutputStream                      +-- OutputStreamWriter
  +-- PrintStream                                 +-- FileWriter(internal)

WHICH TO USE?
- Use BYTE STREAMS for: images (.jpg, .png), audio (.mp3), video (.mp4), archives (.zip), executables (.exe), any binary format
- Use CHARACTER STREAMS for: text files (.txt), source code (.java), web pages (.html, .css), data files (.csv, .xml, .json), configuration files (.properties, .ini)

Summary: Byte streams read/write bytes; character streams read/write text with encoding support.

question: What is JDBC? Explain the steps to connect a Java application to a database.
mainConcepts: JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) is Java's API for connecting to and querying relational databases
Step 1: Load the JDBC driver (Class.forName() — optional in modern JDBC)
Step 2: Establish a connection using DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, password)
Step 3: Create a Statement or PreparedStatement object
Step 4: Execute SQL queries and process the ResultSet
Step 5: Close all resources (Connection, Statement, ResultSet)

question: What is the difference between Statement and PreparedStatement in JDBC?
mainConcepts: PreparedStatement is precompiled and more efficient for repeated execution
PreparedStatement uses parameter placeholders (?) to prevent SQL injection
Statement concatenates SQL strings, making it vulnerable to SQL injection
PreparedStatement handles data type conversion automatically (setInt, setString, etc.)
Statement is suitable for simple, one-time queries with no parameters
PreparedStatement is the recommended choice for production applications

question: What is the ResultSet interface in JDBC? How do you navigate and retrieve data from it?
mainConcepts: ResultSet represents the results of a SQL query — a table of data
ResultSet maintains a cursor pointing to the current row
next() moves the cursor forward and returns false when no more rows exist
Data is retrieved using getter methods: getInt(), getString(), getDouble(), etc.
Columns can be accessed by index (1-based) or by column name
ResultSet is forward-only by default; can be made scrollable with specific parameters

question: What is connection pooling and why is it important in JDBC applications?
mainConcepts: Connection pooling maintains a pool of reusable database connections
Creating a new connection is expensive (network handshake, authentication, memory)
Pooled connections are borrowed, used, and returned to the pool (not closed)
Connection pooling significantly improves performance in multi-user applications
Popular pooling libraries: HikariCP, Apache DBCP, C3P0
DataSource interface with connection pooling is the standard approach in enterprise Java

question: How do you perform CRUD operations using JDBC PreparedStatement?
mainConcepts: CRUD stands for Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four basic database operations
INSERT: executeUpdate() with INSERT SQL
SELECT: executeQuery() returns ResultSet
UPDATE: executeUpdate() with UPDATE SQL
DELETE: executeUpdate() with DELETE SQL
PreparedStatement prevents SQL injection and improves performance

question: Explain the concept of dynamic method dispatch in Java with an example.
mainConcepts: Dynamic method dispatch is the mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved at runtime
JVM determines which method to call based on the actual object type, not the reference type
Enables runtime polymorphism — one interface, multiple implementations
Requires upcasting: parent reference = child object
Only overriding is resolved dynamically (not overloading or fields)
Also called dynamic binding or late binding

question: What is covariant return type in method overriding?
mainConcepts: Covariant return type allows a subclass overriding method to return a more specific (sub)type
Introduced in Java 5
Before Java 5, the return type had to match exactly
Eliminates the need for explicit downcasting after calling overridden methods
Works only with reference types (not primitives)
Example: clone() in Object returns Object, but a subclass can override it to return its own type

question: Explain the instanceof operator in Java. When would you use it?
mainConcepts: instanceof checks whether an object is an instance of a specific class, subclass, or interface
Returns boolean: true if the object is of that type or a subtype
Often used before downcasting to avoid ClassCastException
null instanceof Type always returns false
Can be used with interfaces to check if an object implements a certain interface
Pattern matching for instanceof (Java 16+) allows combining check and cast

question: What is constructor chaining in Java? How does super() and this() work together?
mainConcepts: Constructor chaining is the process of calling one constructor from another constructor
this() calls another constructor in the SAME class
super() calls a constructor in the PARENT class
Both must be the FIRST statement in the constructor
If neither this() nor super() is specified, super() (no-arg) is inserted automatically
Cannot use both this() and super() in the same constructor

question: What is the Java exception hierarchy? Explain Throwable, Exception, Error, and RuntimeException.
mainConcepts: Throwable is the root class of Java's exception hierarchy
Throwable has two main subclasses: Exception and Error
Exception represents conditions an application should handle (checked: must catch/declare)
RuntimeException is a subclass of Exception representing programming bugs (unchecked)
Error represents serious system problems that applications should NOT try to handle
Checked exceptions are everything under Exception except RuntimeException
referenceAnswer:
Java's exception hierarchy is rooted at the Throwable class, which extends Object. It has two main branches: Exception and Error.

EXCEPTION HIERARCHY:

Object
  +-- Throwable
        +-- Exception (checked exceptions — must be handled)
        |     +-- RuntimeException (unchecked — programming bugs)
        |     |     +-- NullPointerException
        |     |     +-- ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        |     |     +-- ArithmeticException
        |     |     +-- IllegalArgumentException
        |     |     +-- NumberFormatException
        |     |     +-- ClassCastException
        |     |     +-- IndexOutOfBoundsException
        |     |
        |     +-- IOException (checked)
        |     |     +-- FileNotFoundException
        |     |     +-- EOFException
        |     |
        |     +-- SQLException (checked)
        |     +-- ClassNotFoundException (checked)
        |     +-- InterruptedException (checked)
        |
        +-- Error (unchecked — serious system problems, NOT for handling)
              +-- OutOfMemoryError
              +-- StackOverflowError
              +-- NoClassDefFoundError
              +-- VirtualMachineError
              +-- AssertionError

1. THROWABLE (root class):
- Provides common methods: getMessage(), printStackTrace(), toString(), getCause()
- Only objects of Throwable or its subclasses can be thrown/caught

2. EXCEPTION:
- Represents conditions that a well-written application should anticipate and handle
- Divided into checked and unchecked exceptions
- CHECKED: Must be caught (try-catch) or declared (throws)
  - IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException, InterruptedException
- UNCHECKED (RuntimeException): Need not be caught or declared
  - NullPointerException, ArithmeticException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException

3. RUNTIMEEXCEPTION:
- Subclass of Exception
- Indicates programming bugs: null checks, division by zero, array overflow, invalid casts
- The compiler does NOT require handling — fixing the bug is the proper approach
- If a NullPointerException occurs, the fix is to check for null, not to wrap in try-catch

4. ERROR:
- Subclass of Throwable (NOT of Exception)
- Indicates serious problems that normal applications should NOT try to catch
- Examples: OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError, VirtualMachineError
- These are external to the application and usually unrecoverable
- Catching an Error is strongly discouraged

CATCH-OR-DECLARE RULE:
Checked exceptions (Exception and subclasses except RuntimeException):
- Must be caught with try-catch OR declared with throws in method signature
- Examples: FileNotFoundException, SQLException, IOException

Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException, Error):
- No compile-time requirement to catch or declare
- Can still be caught, but not required

This design ensures that recoverable errors are explicitly handled, while unrecoverable system problems and programming bugs are not forced to be caught.

question: Can you have a try block without a catch block? Explain the finally block behavior.
mainConcepts: A try block can exist without a catch block, but it must have a finally block
A try block can have multiple catch blocks, a finally block, or both
The finally block ALWAYS executes (even if return, break, continue, or exception occurs)
Only System.exit() or JVM crash can prevent finally from executing
try-with-resources is a special form of try that ensures cleanup without explicit finally
Without a catch, exceptions propagate up the call stack; finally runs before propagation

question: What are wrapper classes in Java? Explain autoboxing and unboxing.
mainConcepts: Wrapper classes convert primitive types into objects (Integer, Double, Boolean, etc.)
Autoboxing is the automatic conversion of a primitive to its wrapper class
Unboxing is the automatic conversion of a wrapper object to its primitive type
Autoboxing/unboxing were introduced in Java 5
Wrapper classes are useful for collections (which only work with objects)
Each primitive type has a corresponding wrapper class with utility methods

question: Explain the ArrayList class in Java. How is it different from an array?
mainConcepts: ArrayList is a resizable array implementation of the List interface (java.util)
Unlike arrays, ArrayList can grow and shrink dynamically
ArrayList stores objects only (not primitives — use wrapper classes)
Arrays have fixed size; ArrayList automatically resizes when needed
ArrayList provides built-in methods: add(), get(), remove(), size(), contains()
ArrayList can be declared with generics to ensure type safety

question: What is the NetBeans GUI Builder? How does it help in creating Swing applications?
mainConcepts: NetBeans GUI Builder (formerly Matisse) is a visual design tool for creating Swing GUIs
Allows drag-and-drop placement of Swing components on a design surface
Generates Java code automatically using GroupLayout
Provides property sheets for configuring component properties
Enables rapid prototyping and visual arrangement
Generated code is in a locked section; manual code goes in customizer methods
referenceAnswer:
The NetBeans GUI Builder (originally called Matisse) is a visual design tool integrated into the NetBeans IDE that allows developers to create Swing-based graphical user interfaces using drag-and-drop, rather than writing layout code manually.

KEY FEATURES:

1. VISUAL DESIGN:
- Drag Swing components (JButton, JLabel, JTextField, etc.) from a palette
- Drop them onto a design surface (JFrame, JPanel, JDialog)
- Visually position and resize components
- See a real-time preview of the GUI appearance

2. AUTOMATIC CODE GENERATION:
- The GUI Builder generates the Java source code automatically
- Generated code uses GroupLayout (but looks complex and is human-unfriendly)
- Generated code is placed in a "locked" section — manually editing is discouraged
- Instead, add code in customizer methods or event handlers

3. PROPERTY SHEET:
- Each selected component shows a property sheet
- Properties like text, font, color, size, enabled state, tooltip can be set visually
- Event handlers can be connected by double-clicking components

4. EVENT HANDLING:
- Double-click a button → NetBeans generates the event handler method skeleton
- The developer adds the action logic inside the generated method
- Avoids writing ActionListener boilerplate manually

5. ADVANTAGES:
- Rapid GUI development — minutes instead of hours
- Accurate visual representation of the final UI
- No manual layout manager math needed
- Consistent look and feel
- Easy component property editing

6. DISADVANTAGES:
- Generated code is verbose and hard to read manually
- Merging changes in version control can be difficult
- Fine-tuning layout sometimes requires understanding GroupLayout
- Limited flexibility compared to hand-coded Swing

TYPICAL WORKFLOW:
1. Create a new JFrame Form in NetBeans
2. Drag components from the Palette to the design area
3. Arrange them visually using alignment guidelines
4. Use the Property Sheet to set text, names, and other properties
5. Right-click → Events → Add event handler (or double-click a button)
6. Write the event-handling logic in the generated method
7. Run the project to test the GUI

WHEN TO USE:
- Rapid prototyping
- Simple to moderately complex GUIs
- Teams where UI designers are not Java experts
- Educational settings (beginners can create GUIs quickly)

WHEN TO HAND-CODE:
- Complex, dynamic layouts
- Performance-critical UIs
- Code that needs fine-grained control
- When the code must be easily maintainable in version control

NetBeans GUI Builder uses GroupLayout by default, which is complex to write manually but very powerful. The builder handles all the constraints automatically.

question: Explain how to use JTextArea, JScrollPane, and JList in Swing applications.
mainConcepts: JTextArea displays/edits multi-line text
JScrollPane provides scrollable viewport for components like JTextArea and JList
JList displays a list of selectable items
JScrollPane is a container that wraps another component and adds scroll bars
JList uses a ListModel to manage data and ListSelectionListener for selection events
JTextArea and JList are often wrapped in JScrollPane for usability

question: What is the File class in Java? Explain common File operations.
mainConcepts: The File class (java.io) represents a file or directory path, not the file content
File objects are used to inspect, create, delete, and rename files and directories
Common methods: exists(), isFile(), isDirectory(), length(), getName(), getPath()
createNewFile() creates an empty file; mkdir() creates a directory
listFiles() returns an array of File objects for directory contents
The File class has constructors for different path formats

question: Explain BufferedWriter and BufferedReader. Why do we wrap them around other streams?
mainConcepts: BufferedReader reads text from a character input stream, buffering for efficient reading
BufferedWriter writes text to a character output stream, buffering for efficient writing
They wrap underlying reader/writer streams to add buffering functionality (Decorator Pattern)
Buffering reduces the number of I/O operations by reading/writing larger chunks
BufferedReader provides readLine() for line-by-line reading
BufferedWriter provides newLine() for platform-independent line separator

question: What are SQL transactions? How do you manage transactions in JDBC?
mainConcepts: A transaction is a group of SQL operations executed as a single unit — all succeed or all fail
ACID properties: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
setAutoCommit(false) starts a transaction; commit() confirms; rollback() undoes
JDBC uses auto-commit mode by default (each SQL is its own transaction)
Savepoints allow partial rollback within a transaction
Transactions maintain database integrity in multi-step operations

question: What is SQL injection? How does PreparedStatement prevent it?
mainConcepts: SQL injection is a security vulnerability where attackers inject malicious SQL into queries
It occurs when user input is concatenated directly into SQL strings
PreparedStatement uses parameterized queries with ? placeholders to separate SQL from data
The database driver escapes and sanitizes parameters automatically
Always use PreparedStatement for queries with user input
Never trust user input — validate and parameterize
referenceAnswer:
SQL injection is a code injection technique where an attacker inserts malicious SQL statements into an application's query by exploiting unsanitized user input. It is one of the most common and dangerous web application vulnerabilities.

HOW SQL INJECTION WORKS (VULNERABLE CODE):

Suppose we have a login form:
String user = request.getParameter("username");   // user-provided input
String pass = request.getParameter("password");   // user-provided input

// VULNERABLE - string concatenation:
String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '" + user + "' AND password = '" + pass + "'";
Statement stmt = conn.createStatement();
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery(sql);

ATTACK SCENARIOS:

1. LOGIN BYPASS:
User enters: username = admin' -- 
Resulting SQL: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' -- ' AND password = '...'
The -- comments out the password check. Attacker logs in as admin!

2. DATA THEFT:
User enters: username = ' OR 1=1 --
Resulting SQL: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR 1=1 -- ' AND password = '...'
Returns ALL users. Attacker gets all usernames and hashed passwords.

3. DATA DESTRUCTION:
User enters: username = '; DROP TABLE users; --
Resulting SQL: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ''; DROP TABLE users; -- ' ...'
Multiple statements — deletes the entire users table!

4. UNION-BASED DATA EXTRACTION:
User enters: ' UNION SELECT username, password FROM admin_users --
Attacker extracts data from other tables.

HOW PREPAREDSTATEMENT PREVENTS SQL INJECTION:

PreparedStatement separates SQL structure from data using parameter placeholders (?):

String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?";
PreparedStatement pstmt = conn.prepareStatement(sql);
pstmt.setString(1, user);   // user input is treated as DATA, not SQL
pstmt.setString(2, pass);
ResultSet rs = pstmt.executeQuery();

Even if user enters: admin' OR 1=1 --
The database treats it as a literal string value for the username field, not as SQL code.
The query effectively searches for a user whose username is literally: admin' OR 1=1 --

WHY PREPAREDSTATEMENT IS SAFE:

1. PARAMETER SEPARATION:
The SQL statement is compiled FIRST (with ? placeholders). Then parameter values are sent SEPARATELY. The database's SQL parser never sees the user input as SQL code.

2. AUTOMATIC ESCAPING:
The JDBC driver automatically escapes special characters. Quotes, semicolons, and SQL keywords in parameter values are escaped so they are treated as literal data.

3. TYPE SAFETY:
setString(), setInt(), setDouble() enforce the expected data type. An attacker cannot inject SQL through an integer parameter.

DEMONSTRATION OF SAFETY:
// Even if user input contains malicious SQL:
String userInput = "' OR 1=1; DROP TABLE users; --";

// SAFE:
PreparedStatement pstmt = conn.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ?");
pstmt.setString(1, userInput);
// Executes as: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ''' OR 1=1; DROP TABLE users; --'
// Just searches for a user with that weird username — NO SQL injection!

ADDITIONAL DEFENSES:
1. Input validation: Validate that input matches expected format (email, phone, alphanumeric)
2. Least privilege: Database user should have minimal necessary permissions (no DROP for app user)
3. Stored procedures: Can also prevent injection if parameterized properly
4. ORM frameworks: JPA/Hibernate use parameterized queries internally
5. Never use Statement for queries with any user-provided data

GOLDEN RULE:
NEVER concatenate user input into SQL strings. ALWAYS use PreparedStatement (or equivalent parameterized query).

question: What is the difference between equals() and == in Java?
mainConcepts: == compares primitive values or object REFERENCES (memory addresses)
equals() compares the actual CONTENT or STATE of objects
For primitives (int, double, etc.), use == (equals() doesn't apply)
For objects, == checks if two references point to the same object in memory
The default equals() in Object class uses == (same as reference check)
String, Integer, and other built-in classes override equals() to compare content

question: What are the enhanced for loop (for-each) and varargs in Java?
mainConcepts: Enhanced for loop iterates over arrays and collections without using indexes
Syntax: for (Type item : iterable) { }
Simpler and less error-prone than traditional indexed loops
Varargs allows methods to accept variable number of arguments of the same type
Varargs syntax: Type... parameterName (internally treated as an array)
Varargs parameter must be the last parameter in the method

question: How do you copy a binary file using FileInputStream and FileOutputStream with buffering?
mainConcepts: FileInputStream reads bytes from a file; FileOutputStream writes bytes to a file
BufferedInputStream/BufferedOutputStream add buffering for performance
Reading/writing byte arrays in chunks improves performance over single-byte operations
Use try-with-resources for automatic cleanup
A buffer size of 4KB–64KB is typical for file copy operations
File.copy() (java.nio.file) is simplest for basic file copy in modern Java

question: Explain JComboBox and JCheckBox in Swing. When would you use each?
mainConcepts: JComboBox is a drop-down list for selecting ONE option from multiple choices
JCheckBox is a box that can be checked/unchecked for boolean/toggle options
JComboBox is space-efficient and good for many mutually exclusive options
JCheckBox is good for independent on/off options (multiple can be selected)
JComboBox uses ActionListener or ItemListener for selection events
JCheckBox uses ItemListener or ActionListener for state changes
